title: Lesson 419
transcript: OK, I just gave you a fairly high level overview of virtual machines and hypervisors. I want to go into a little more detail on these things. I want to use my light board here and draw some stuff for you. because there's some terminology that I'm gonna highlight here that's important to know for the exam. So, right, the whole idea here of like, this is all based high level on the concept of abstraction. Abstraction is the idea of hiding the underlying complexity. An example of abstraction is virtualization. Virtualization is where you're creating a virtual version of something. And a perfect example of that is hypervisors and virtual machines. So let's go through that. I'm going to cover some of the stuff I covered in the previous video, but I'll do a longer-windowed explanation here. Let's start at the hardware level. So this is going to be our computer system. This is going to be the hardware of our computer system here. So this will be like the CPU and RAM and the network interface card and a bunch of other stuff as well. But we've got the hardware. And in a typical computer system, the major piece of software that will then be running on that underlying hardware is, of course, the operating system. And let's say we have the Windows operating system installed here. So we'll have Windows. And then on top of Windows, you can then run processes, programs, applications, like, say, I don't know, Zoom. So this is a very basic generic view of a computer system. You've got your underlying hardware, you've got your operating system, and then you've got your application running on top of it here. Now let's say for some reason, you know what? We don't want to just run Zoom here. We also want to be able to run this program called Sketch. If you've never come across Sketch, it's a user interface design tool. and rather problematically, Sketch does not run on Windows. It only runs on Mac OS. So what we might wanna do here, if we wanna be able to run Sketch, we're gonna need to also install Mac OS on our computer system here. And now the question is, is this going to work? If we try and install Windows, one operating system, and Mac OS, another operating system, on the same underlying computer system, and then we power that computer system on, Are both of these operating systems going to happily run side by side? Will they both happily run the way I've drawn this? And the answer is unequivocally, no. Why not? Well, because one of the major jobs of an operating system is to have total and complete control of all the underlying hardware. And so if you have two operating systems that are both trying to have total and complete control of all the underlying hardware, this will never work. These two operating systems will just completely conflict with each other. The system would never boot up. It would never work. You cannot do this. And yet we do this all the time. We have multiple operating systems running on one underlying physical server or computer system all the time. How? The answer is we have to create a layer of abstraction, a layer of virtualization between the operating systems and the underlying hardware. So what we do here, is we add an additional piece of software. And that's important to highlight here, right? This is a layer of software. We're not adding any new hardware here. So what we're gonna do is we're gonna add an additional piece of software here. And on top of this piece of software, We're then going to run our two operating systems. So here's we're going to have Windows and we're going to have Mac OS here. And then of course, on top of Windows, we can run our Zoom application. And then on top of Mac OS, we can run Sketch, like that. By introducing this additional layer of software here, we can now run these two operating systems simultaneously. So what magical piece of software allows us to do this? It's called the hypervisor. So the hypervisor is a piece of software that allows us to run multiple operating systems simultaneously. How? Well, the way I like to describe this rather comically is the hypervisor is essentially a giant liar. Because what's the hypervisor saying to Windows? It's saying, hey, Windows, you're the greatest operating system ever. You're in total and complete control of all the underlying hardware. And so Windows is like, yeah. I'm the greatest operating system ever. I'm in total and complete control. And so Windows happily runs because it thinks it's in total and complete control of all the underlying hardware, but it's not. What's actually happening here is the hypervisor is, the technical term here is it's performing emulation. Emulation is creating a virtual version of, say, a piece of hardware. So what the hypervisor is doing is it's emulating the CPU. It's emulating RAM. It's emulating the network interface card. In other words, it's creating a virtual CPU. virtual RAM, virtual NIC. And so what Windows is actually controlling here is not the underlying hardware. Instead, what Windows is controlling is the virtual CPU. and the virtual RAM, and then virtual network interface card. But Windows doesn't know that. For all Windows is concerned, it thinks it's controlling real hardware, but it's not. It's actually just controlling virtual hardware that's being emulated by the hypervisor. And so that's why Windows happily runs, because it thinks it's in total and complete control of all the underlying hardware, but it's not. And of course, it's the same story for macOS, right? The hypervisor is lying to macOS as well. It's like the hypervisor saying, hey, macOS, you're the greatest operating system ever. You're in total and complete control. And so macOS is like, yeah. But of course, what's happening here again is that Mac OS is not controlling the true underlying hardware. It's just controlling virtual versions of it. So this allows us to run multiple operating systems simultaneously. So now let's get into a ton of terminology here. So When you have an operating system and some applications running on top of a hypervisor, you call this here, this piece right here, you call this a virtual machine. Some other names for VMs that you might come across. Another common name for a virtual machine is an instance. or sometimes also referred to as a guest. So just remember that. Two commonly used synonymous terms for a virtual machine is instance or guest. So VM, instance, guest, these terms are all pervasively used in the industry to refer to this, right? An operating system with some applications running on top of it, running atop a hypervisor. And of course, how many virtual machines do we have here? We got VM1 and then this here is VM2. Virtual machine number two. So we have two virtual machines running here. An interesting question to contemplate is, to what degree can this hypervisor see what these virtual machines are up to? To what degree can this hypervisor see what they're doing? And the answer is, the hypervisor essentially has total and complete visibility into whatever these VMs are doing. Because think about it, right? Anytime this virtual machine sends any command to the CPU, any command to RAM, any data to RAM, any data across the network, any data to the hard drive, when this virtual machine does anything All of that is going through the virtual hardware that the hypervisor is emulating. So the hypervisor has basically total and complete visibility into what these VMs are doing. Here's why I'm pointing this out. Another less commonly used name for a hypervisor is actually a VM... The reason VM monitors, because if you think about it, to what degree can this hypervisor see what these VMs are doing? completely the hypervisor is able to monitor what the vms are up to so i point this out because this terminology is sometimes on the exam and it's not as commonly used in the industry so just remember another name for the hypervisor is VM monitor, okay? More terminology. Each virtual machine, right? Which is an operating system with some applications running on top of it. Each virtual machine is typically going to expect to have its own one or more of its own hard drives. So attached to each virtual machine is typically going to be one or more virtual hard drives. Here's another bit of terminology you might come across. In this context, another name for a virtual hard drive is also a volume. That term gets used for other things in other places. But in this context related to virtual machines, The term volume refers to a virtual hard drive. So remember that. Another name for a virtual hard drive is a volume. Okay. or terminology. Let's say for some reason, let's say I wanna create 10 identical virtual machines. Like let's say I wanna create a cluster of 10 Apache web servers. The inefficient way of doing this would be to create each one manually. So I'd create a new virtual machine. I'd install some flavor of Linux. I'd install the Apache web server. I'd update everything, set up the right user access controls. configure it properly, blah, blah, blah. It's going to take me a good chunk of time, a few hours probably, to do this, to set up one virtual machine. Now, the inefficient way of doing this would just be to do the same thing over and over again. If we want 10 virtual machines, you could just do that process nine more times. That's going to take you probably a few days, or at least a few hours. So that is not an efficient way of doing this. This is where images come in. An image is essentially a, listen carefully here, an image is essentially a pre-built ready to be deployed virtual machine. So the idea here is you take the time to essentially create the perfect virtual machine. You set up the operating system with all the right patches and configure it correctly. You install the Apache web server. You set all of that up correctly. You set up all the right user credentials, whatever you need to do. to properly configure, lock down, harden, and create the perfect ready to be deployed virtual machine. That's what an image is. Companies will typically store these images in repositories. And by the way, when you have an image that's like the perfect image ready to be deployed, you'll often hear the term like that's your golden image. The golden image is like the perfect one that's ready to be put into production. So you'll have a repository storing a bunch of these different golden images. And now, if I wanted to create 10 identical virtual machines, all I have to do is I have to take the time to create this image. So that's still going to take a bit of time. But now that I have this image ready, It's incredibly easy to stand up 10 virtual machines. I basically just copy this image 10 times, which creates 10 virtual machines, power them all on, and boom. Once I've got the image made, within a few seconds, I can have a whole cluster of identical virtual machines running on, say, a bunch of different hypervisors across my environment. And companies will maintain repositories of these different images. So if someone ever says, oh, I need a web server, or I need this type of server, there you go. Just pull the image out of the repository. You can deploy it in seconds. So that's an image, a pre-built, ready to be deployed virtual machine. One final bit of terminology here. This is going back to, again, what visibility does the hypervisor have in the VM? Total visibility. So one of the cool things that the hypervisor can do is create what's called a snapshot. When I think snapshot, I always think Polaroid cameras. If you ever played with a Polaroid camera, right? You take a picture of something, instantly the film would roll out. You just have to kind of like shake it for a few seconds and then boom, you'd have a picture. It was like an instant picture of something. That's what a snapshot is. It's like a Polaroid picture. A snapshot is, listen carefully here, a snapshot is a moment in time capture of a VM. And snapshots are highly configurable. And what you can do is you can configure a snapshot to capture every bit of data related to a virtual machine at a moment in time. Any of the data that's in the virtual CPU's cache and registers, any of the data that's in the virtual RAM, any of the data that's on the virtual volumes, hard drives, literally any bit of data related to a virtual machine, a snapshot can be configured to capture all of that at a moment in time. So a snapshot is a moment in time capture of a running virtual machine. Snapshots can be super useful for a bunch of different reasons. One useful, one use of a snapshot is let's say you're about to patch this VM. You of course should have done a bunch of testing ahead of time and you should be as sure as possible that when you patch this VM, you're not going to break anything, but you know, stuff still happens. So what you could do is right before you patch this VM, you could take a snapshot and store that snapshot. You can then patch the VM. And if you've discovered like, uh-oh, the patch has broken this VM, what you can do is basically just restore that snapshot on the hypervisor, press play again, and that virtual machine will resume from right where it was when you took the snapshot. So it can be useful for patching. Another really useful, really great use of snapshots is in forensic investigations. If you discover this VM has been compromised, you can take a snapshot of the VM, which is a moment in time capture, right? potentially everything related to the VM. As long as the snapshot is taken at the right time, it can be extremely useful for forensic purposes. So that leads to one more question that you might see on the exam. Let's say you wanted to perform a forensic investigation of this virtual machine. What would be most useful to get your hands on? The image, the snapshot, or the volume? And the answer is the snapshot, because the snapshot captures everything related to this virtual machine at a moment in time, including, as long as it's configured correctly, including everything in the volume. So the snapshot would be the most useful thing to get your hands on if you wanted to perform a forensic analysis. Let's say the snapshot wasn't available though. What would be the next most useful thing? The image or the volume? And the answer is the volume. Why? Well, the volume, I mean, think about, this is not quite correct, but think about it this way. The volume is like the hard, like let's say this is a physical server. The volume is the hard drive. And of course, the hard drive contains all the data from the running system. And it's not quite correct, but you could kind of think of the image as being like the Windows install CD, right? Like the Windows installation CD It's not totally useless for forensics purposes, but pretty close, right? Because an image is not going to tell you anything about an actual running system. Because an image is a pre-built, ready to be deployed system. So the only thing the image would tell you is, let's say this VM got hacked, you might look at your image and go, oh, OK, this image has this vulnerability in it. So that's probably how this VM got hacked. But you wouldn't know for sure. A volume, that's the hard drive from the VM, it would contain log file data, all sorts of other useful stuff that you could look at for forensic purposes. So if you had to choose between image and volume, For forensic purposes, volume is way more useful. Snapshot is the most useful. OK, there you go. That's some deeper terminology about hypervisors and VMs. We're going to cover them again just briefly when we talk about cloud computing. And I'll go into a little more detail on the different types of hypervisors. But I thought this would be useful just to go into a little more detail of some of the terminology here around virtual machines.
